note: Effort, cost, and time estimates are directional. Real numbers depend on model choice, agent retries, infrastructure, and benchmark version. Use this planner to compare evaluation options before committing resources.

agent_types.name: Coding / software engineering
agent_types.description: Agents that write, edit, debug, and ship code in repositories.

agent_types.name: General assistant / copilot
agent_types.description: Multi-purpose agents that answer questions, browse, and use tools.

agent_types.name: Web / browser automation
agent_types.description: Agents that navigate websites, fill forms, and complete browser tasks.

agent_types.name: Desktop / OS automation
agent_types.description: Agents that control apps, files, and OS-level workflows.

agent_types.name: Multi-agent / orchestration
agent_types.description: Teams of agents that delegate, coordinate, and share state.

agent_types.name: Voice / conversational
agent_types.description: Agents that handle real-time speech, turn-taking, and call flows.

agent_types.name: Domain-specific (math, science, security, finance)
agent_types.description: Agents tuned for a specialized field with narrow, high-accuracy tasks.

readiness_levels.name: Prototype / demo
readiness_levels.description: Need a quick signal that the agent can do the task at all.

readiness_levels.name: Iteration / regression testing
readiness_levels.description: Need fast, cheap feedback to compare prompts, models, or tool changes.

readiness_levels.name: Pre-release validation
readiness_levels.description: Need a trustworthy score to decide if the agent is ready for users.

readiness_levels.name: Leaderboard / public benchmark
readiness_levels.description: Need a credible number for marketing, investors, or research publication.

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench
benchmarks.what: Fix real GitHub issues end-to-end in popular Python repositories.
benchmarks.pros: Industry standard for real-world coding agents; tests repo-level reasoning.
benchmarks.cons: Expensive; slow; public issues can lead to overfitting.

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench Verified
benchmarks.what: Curated 500-instance subset with confirmed problem/solution pairs.
benchmarks.pros: More reliable than full SWE-bench; good for vendor comparison.
benchmarks.cons: Still costly; may not match your internal codebase.

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench Lite
benchmarks.what: Smaller, simpler subset designed for faster iteration.
benchmarks.pros: Cheaper and faster than full SWE-bench; good CI signal.
benchmarks.cons: Easier; may not generalize to full production issues.

benchmarks.name: HumanEval
benchmarks.what: 164 hand-written function-level programming problems with unit tests.
benchmarks.pros: Fast, cheap, reproducible; great for model comparison.
benchmarks.cons: Toy problems; no repo-level reasoning.

benchmarks.name: MBPP
benchmarks.what: Crowd-sourced Python programming problems.
benchmarks.pros: Larger and more varied than HumanEval.
benchmarks.cons: Simpler than real-world coding; limited scope.

benchmarks.name: GAIA
benchmarks.what: 466 real-world assistant tasks requiring reasoning, browsing, and tool use.
benchmarks.pros: Strong signal for general assistant ability; human-aligned difficulty.
benchmarks.cons: Some tasks need live tools; leaderboard costs add up.

benchmarks.name: MMLU / MMLU-Pro
benchmarks.what: Multiple-choice questions spanning STEM, humanities, and social sciences.
benchmarks.pros: Standardized; easy to run; good for model ranking.
benchmarks.cons: Static knowledge, not agent behavior.

benchmarks.name: LiveBench
benchmarks.what: Questions with answers that change over time to reduce memorization.
benchmarks.pros: Harder to game; tests current knowledge.
benchmarks.cons: Limited coverage; not a full agent test.

benchmarks.name: WebArena
benchmarks.what: 812 reproducible tasks across self-hosted web apps.
benchmarks.pros: Controlled environment; tests realistic web interaction.
benchmarks.cons: Setup heavy; simulated sites differ from live web.

benchmarks.name: WebVoyager
benchmarks.what: Tasks on live websites using screenshots and actions.
benchmarks.pros: Tests real-world resilience: layout changes, captchas, JS.
benchmarks.cons: Noisy; slow; live sites can break evals.

benchmarks.name: Mind2Web
benchmarks.what: Diverse web tasks across 137 sites in 31 domains.
benchmarks.pros: Broad domain coverage; good cross-site generalization test.
benchmarks.cons: Annotation-based; less end-to-end than WebArena.

benchmarks.name: OSWorld
benchmarks.what: 369 tasks across Windows, Ubuntu, and macOS requiring GUI + API reasoning.
benchmarks.pros: Tests real desktop automation; multimodal.
benchmarks.cons: Requires VM infrastructure; slow to evaluate.

benchmarks.name: Windows Agent Arena
benchmarks.what: 154 tasks across Windows applications and system settings.
benchmarks.pros: Focused on Windows enterprise automation scenarios.
benchmarks.cons: Windows-specific; smaller coverage than OSWorld.

benchmarks.name: AgentBench
benchmarks.what: Eight environments spanning reasoning, decision making, coding, and embodied tasks.
benchmarks.pros: Holistic view of agent ability across settings.
benchmarks.cons: Complex setup; not a single narrow score.

benchmarks.name: BenchMAX
benchmarks.what: Tasks designed for agent teams with role specialization.
benchmarks.pros: Directly measures delegation and coordination.
benchmarks.cons: Newer benchmark; fewer public results.

benchmarks.name: SimpleQA
benchmarks.what: Fact-checking benchmark with 4,326 short, diverse questions.
benchmarks.pros: Tests factual correctness and calibration; cheap.
benchmarks.cons: Not agentic; limited to question answering.

benchmarks.name: HellaSwag
benchmarks.what: Commonsense reasoning benchmark for everyday situations.
benchmarks.pros: Fast benchmark for commonsense reasoning.
benchmarks.cons: Dated; easy for modern models; not agentic.

benchmarks.name: GSM8K / MATH
benchmarks.what: Grade-school and competition-level math word problems.
benchmarks.pros: Clear right/wrong scoring; cheap to run.
benchmarks.cons: Text-only; not agentic unless paired with tools.

benchmarks.name: CYBER
benchmarks.what: Capture-the-flag style tasks for offensive security agents.
benchmarks.pros: Practical security scenarios; strong capability signal.
benchmarks.cons: Niche; dangerous if misused.

benchmarks.name: SciWorld
benchmarks.what: Text-based scientific experiments in a simulated lab environment.
benchmarks.pros: Tests hypothesis-driven reasoning and experimentation.
benchmarks.cons: Niche domain; simulator complexity.

benchmarks.name: CoQA
benchmarks.what: Conversational question-answering benchmark with context.
benchmarks.pros: Tests conversation memory and context handling.
benchmarks.cons: Not real-time voice; QA only.

benchmarks.name: DSTC11 / MultiWOZ
benchmarks.what: Task-oriented dialogue benchmark for booking, scheduling, and info lookup.
benchmarks.pros: Standard for dialogue state tracking and task completion.
benchmarks.cons: Text-based; ignores voice latency and ASR errors.

benchmarks.name: Air-Bench
benchmarks.what: Speech-language model benchmark with audio inputs.
benchmarks.pros: Tests audio understanding and spoken QA.
benchmarks.cons: Does not cover live telephony or turn-taking quality.

benchmarks.name: ToolBench
benchmarks.what: API-based tool-use benchmark with 16k+ tool use instances.
benchmarks.pros: Large coverage of API tool use.
benchmarks.cons: Simulated APIs; may not match real API behavior.

benchmarks.name: API-Bank
benchmarks.what: Benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on API calls and planning.
benchmarks.pros: Focuses on API selection and chained tool use.
benchmarks.cons: Smaller than ToolBench; API set is synthetic.
